[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that newClient() does not call any equivalent of client.SetMaxTransactionFee() after client.SetOperator(), leaving the SDK's default uncapped, so that a network fee schedule upgrade that raises fees causes all pinger transactions to be charged at the new higher rate without any application-level rejection?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.Client returned by newClient() inherits the SDK's default max transaction fee (which may be orders of magnitude higher than the actual transfer amount of cfg.amountTinybar), allowing nodes to charge fees that dwarf the intended transfer value, violating the design parameter that fees should be proportional to transfer size?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the config struct has no maxTransactionFee field, meaning there is no design-level parameter to enforce a fee ceiling, and the hiero.Client created by newClient() will accept whatever fee the network charges up to the SDK default, allowing fee modification outside any application-defined parameter?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/sdk_client.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: newClient()] Can an unprivileged external user who can set environment variables inject a crafted HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_ID value that resolves to a different account, causing client.SetOperator() to associate the operator key with an unintended account, and then exploit fee schedule differences for that account to modify effective transaction fees?
